---
title: "Exercise 4.1 — Prove the Standard Vector Norms Are Norms"
subtitle: "Numerical Analysis 2026 — Lecture 4"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

## Problem

Prove that the following three functions are vector norms on
$\mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
\|x\|_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i|,
\qquad
\|x\|_2 = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i|^2\right)^{1/2},
\qquad
\|x\|_\infty = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n}|x_i|.
$$

For each norm, verify the three norm properties:

1. Positivity:
   $$
   \|x\| \geq 0,\qquad \|x\| = 0 \Longleftrightarrow x = 0.
   $$
2. Homogeneity:
   $$
   \|\alpha x\| = |\alpha|\|x\|.
   $$
3. Triangle inequality:
   $$
   \|x+y\| \leq \|x\|+\|y\|.
   $$

::: {.callout-warning}
AI is **not recommended** for the proof itself. You may use AI to convert
your handwritten equations into LaTeX, but the mathematical reasoning
should be yours.
:::

---

## 1. Proof for $\ell^1$

### Positivity

> **Your answer:**
>
>Since \(|x_i|\ge 0\) for every \(i\),

\[
\|x\|_1=\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|\ge 0.
\]

Also, \(\|x\|_1=0\) means

\[
\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|=0.
\]

Since every term is nonnegative, this is possible only when
\(|x_i|=0\) for all \(i\). Therefore \(x_i=0\) for all \(i\), so
\(x=0\). Conversely, if \(x=0\), then \(\|x\|_1=0\).

### Homogeneity

> **Your answer:**
>
> For any scalar \(\alpha\),

\[
\|\alpha x\|_1
=
\sum_{i=1}^n |\alpha x_i|
=
\sum_{i=1}^n |\alpha||x_i|
=
|\alpha|\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|
=
|\alpha|\|x\|_1.
\]

### Triangle inequality

> **Your answer:**
>
> Using the triangle inequality for real numbers,

\[
|x_i+y_i|\le |x_i|+|y_i|.
\]

Therefore,

\[
\|x+y\|_1
=
\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i+y_i|
\le
\sum_{i=1}^n (|x_i|+|y_i|)
=
\|x\|_1+\|y\|_1.
\]

---

## 2. Proof for $\ell^2$

For the triangle inequality, use the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality:

$$
\left|\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i\right|
\leq
\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i^2\right)^{1/2}
\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i^2\right)^{1/2}.
$$

### Positivity

> **Your answer:**
>
>Since \(|x_i|^2\ge 0\) for every \(i\),

\[
\|x\|_2
=
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|^2\right)^{1/2}
\ge 0.
\]

Also, \(\|x\|_2=0\) means

\[
\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|^2=0.
\]

Since each term is nonnegative, this happens only when
\(|x_i|^2=0\) for all \(i\). Thus \(x_i=0\) for all \(i\), so \(x=0\).
Conversely, if \(x=0\), then \(\|x\|_2=0\).

### Homogeneity

> **Your answer:**
>
> For any scalar \(\alpha\),

\[
\|\alpha x\|_2
=
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n |\alpha x_i|^2\right)^{1/2}
=
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n |\alpha|^2 |x_i|^2\right)^{1/2}
=
|\alpha|
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|^2\right)^{1/2}
=
|\alpha|\|x\|_2.
\]

### Triangle inequality

> **Your answer:**
>
> First,

\[
\|x+y\|_2^2
=
\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i+y_i|^2.
\]

Expanding,

\[
\|x+y\|_2^2
=
\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|^2
+
2\left|\sum_{i=1}^n x_i y_i\right|
+
\sum_{i=1}^n |y_i|^2.
\]

By the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality,

\[
\sum_{i=1}^n x_i y_i
\le
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n |x_i|^2\right)^{1/2}
\left(\sum_{i=1}^n |y_i|^2\right)^{1/2}
=
\|x\|_2\|y\|_2.
\]

Therefore,

\[
\|x+y\|_2^2
\le
\|x\|_2^2+2\|x\|_2\|y\|_2+\|y\|_2^2
=
(\|x\|_2+\|y\|_2)^2.
\]

Taking square roots gives

\[
\|x+y\|_2
\le
\|x\|_2+\|y\|_2.
\]

---

## 3. Proof for $\ell^\infty$

### Positivity

> **Your answer:**
>
> Since \(|x_i|\ge 0\) for every \(i\),

\[
\|x\|_\infty=\max_{1\le i\le n}|x_i|\ge 0.
\]

Also, \(\|x\|_\infty=0\) means

\[
\max_{1\le i\le n}|x_i|=0.
\]

Since all \(|x_i|\ge 0\), this is possible only when
\(|x_i|=0\) for all \(i\). Therefore \(x=0\). Conversely, if \(x=0\),
then \(\|x\|_\infty=0\).

### Homogeneity

> **Your answer:**
>
> For any scalar \(\alpha\),

\[
\|\alpha x\|_\infty
=
\max_i |\alpha x_i|
=
\max_i |\alpha||x_i|
=
|\alpha|\max_i |x_i|
=
|\alpha|\|x\|_\infty.
\]

### Triangle inequality

> **Your answer:**
>
> For each component,

\[
|x_i+y_i|\le |x_i|+|y_i|.
\]

Also,

\[
|x_i|\le \|x\|_\infty,
\qquad
|y_i|\le \|y\|_\infty.
\]

Thus,

\[
|x_i+y_i|
\le
\|x\|_\infty+\|y\|_\infty
\]

for every \(i\). Taking the maximum over \(i\), we get

\[
\|x+y\|_\infty
=
\max_i |x_i+y_i|
\le
\|x\|_\infty+\|y\|_\infty.
\]
